key_insight: In rough lubricated contacts, two-component lubrication outperforms single fluids by preserving plug flow to lower speeds and minimizing friction, wear, and material transfer, while single fluids can develop cavitation-driven stress release after asperity collisions.
engineering_relevance: For boundary and mixed-lubrication design, the results suggest that fluid pairing and interfacial geometry can be as important as viscosity in controlling friction, load support, and wear.
method: Non-equilibrium molecular dynamics of sliding between rough, deformable surfaces
Compared water, n-dodecane, and immiscible two-fluid lubrication under identical geometry and loading
Analyzed friction, load-bearing capacity, plug flow, cavitation, and wear-particle formation
evidence_signal: The abstract reports direct simulation comparisons showing water has stronger speed dependence but lower load support than n-dodecane, while the mixed lubricant achieves the lowest friction and material transfer and best preserves plug flow.
watch_item: These findings come from molecular dynamics under specific rough-contact conditions, so transfer to real systems may depend on surface chemistry, roughness, and fluid immiscibility.
paper_title: Plug Flow and Cavitation in Rough Lubricated Contacts: Molecular Dynamics of Single- vs. Two-Component Fluids
